coredata.dc:title: Hydriding/dehydriding properties of nanocrystalline Mg87Ni3Al3M7 (M=Ti, Mn, Ce, La) alloys prepared by ball milling
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2004 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Spassov, T.
Rangelova, V.
Solsona, P.
Baró, M.D.
Zander, D.
Köster, U.
coredata.dc:description: Nanocrystalline Mg87Ni3Al3M7 (M=Ti, Mn, Mm; Mm=Ce, La-rich mischmetal) hydrides were synthesized by reactive mechanical milling (RMM) in hydrogen atmosphere. Different degrees of the alloys hydrogenation were achieved varying the time of milling at a hydrogen pressure of 5atm. The influence of the alloying elements (Ti, Mn, Mm) on the amount of the hydrides formed during RMM as well as on the temperature and enthalpy of hydrogen desorption was determined. The amount of hydride formed during RMM was found to be almost the same in the three alloys (∼35wt.%); for the Ti containing alloy it was slightly higher. The Ti containing alloy reveals the lowest temperature of hydrogen desorption (∼210°C) and the hydride in the alloy with Mn decomposes at the highest temperature (∼240°C) among the three alloys studied. The enthalpy of H-desorption is highest for the sample alloyed with Mm (Ce, La), 70–72kJ/mol; the Ti and Mn containing magnesium alloys have similar enthalpies of hydride decomposition, 56–60kJ/mol. The alloys studied reveal higher equilibrium pressures of hydrogen absorption compared to nanocrystalline magnesium, indicating some thermodynamic destabilization of the hydride as a result of the alloying. Hydrogen absorption kinetics were studied at isothermal conditions at different temperatures and an influence of the alloying element was observed. The hydrogen absorption in the Ti containing alloy is substantially faster than in the alloys with Mn and Mm.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Magnesium alloys
Nanocrystalline
Reactive mechanical milling
Hydriding/dehydriding

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Morphology and microstructure of the as-milled alloys
Thermal behavior of the as-milled alloys

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: T.S. thanks the Alexander von Humboldt Foundation (Germany) for a grant and for the JEOL 5510 SEM donation. The work has been supported by the EU HPRN-CT-2002-00208.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM photographs of the as-milled material (a) and of an agglomerate (b).
XRD patterns after different times of milling of Mg(87at.%)–Al(3at.%)–Ni(3at.%)–Mn(7at.%) under hydrogen atmosphere.
Thermogravimetric curves of the as-milled alloys (after RMM in hydrogen atmosphere for 15h).

Amount of hydride formed by RMM under hydrogen atmosphere as a function of milling time for Mg87Ni3Al3Mn7 ((
) selected particle-size fraction <20μm).
DSC scans of the as-milled alloys (15h RMM).

PCI of hydrogen absorption: (a) hydriding at 300°C (■) Mg87Ni3Al3Mn7 (I cycle), (□) Mg87Ni3Al3Mn7 (II cycle), (●) Mg87Ni3Al3Ti7 (I cycle) and (▴) Mg87Ni3Al3Mm7 (I cycle) and (b) hydriding/dehydriding of Mg87Ni3Al3Mn7 (I cycle).
Isothermal kinetics of hydrogen absorption for Mg87Ni3Al3Ti7 at two different temperatures.

article.ce:title: Hydriding/dehydriding properties of nanocrystalline Mg87Ni3Al3M7 (M=Ti, Mn, Ce, La) alloys prepared by ball milling

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, University of Sofia “St. Kl. Ohridski”, 1 J. Bourchier Str., 1126 Sofia, Bulgaria

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Departament de Física, Facultat de Ciències, Universitat Autònoma de Barcelona, 08193 Bellaterra, Spain

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biochemical and Chemical Engineering, University of Dortmund, Dortmund, Germany

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nanocrystalline Mg87Ni3Al3M7 (M=Ti, Mn, Mm; Mm=Ce, La-rich mischmetal) hydrides were synthesized by reactive mechanical milling (RMM) in hydrogen atmosphere. Different degrees of the alloys hydrogenation were achieved varying the time of milling at a hydrogen pressure of 5atm. The influence of the alloying elements (Ti, Mn, Mm) on the amount of the hydrides formed during RMM as well as on the temperature and enthalpy of hydrogen desorption was determined. The amount of hydride formed during RMM was found to be almost the same in the three alloys (∼35wt.%); for the Ti containing alloy it was slightly higher. The Ti containing alloy reveals the lowest temperature of hydrogen desorption (∼210°C) and the hydride in the alloy with Mn decomposes at the highest temperature (∼240°C) among the three alloys studied. The enthalpy of H-desorption is highest for the sample alloyed with Mm (Ce, La), 70–72kJ/mol; the Ti and Mn containing magnesium alloys have similar enthalpies of hydride decomposition, 56–60kJ/mol. The alloys studied reveal higher equilibrium pressures of hydrogen absorption compared to nanocrystalline magnesium, indicating some thermodynamic destabilization of the hydride as a result of the alloying. Hydrogen absorption kinetics were studied at isothermal conditions at different temperatures and an influence of the alloying element was observed. The hydrogen absorption in the Ti containing alloy is substantially faster than in the alloys with Mn and Mm.

ce:section.ce:para: The interest to investigate magnesium and magnesium rich alloys for hydrogen storage is due to their high hydrogen storage capacity, light weight and low cost. Their relatively high hydrogen desorption temperature and enthalpy (high stability of the hydrides) and sluggish sorption kinetics are the main drawbacks of these materials. Although a number of studies are devoted to the thermodynamics and kinetics of hydriding/dehydriding in Mg-based alloys and composites still work is necessary to reduce the dehydriding temperature and enthalpy, while keeping a high hydrogen capacity.

Nanocrystalline Mg alloys prepared by ball milling (BM) or RMM possess good hydrogen sorption kinetics. Full hydriding/dehydriding at 300°C and moderate pressure can be achieved after several minutes. The H-sorption kinetics of nanocrystalline Mg have been further enhanced by adding different catalysts, e.g. Nb, Nb2O5 [1–4]. Alloying magnesium with elements like Ti, Zr, Mn, Al, Mm and V improves the thermodynamics and kinetics of the hydriding/dehydriding process. Addition of e.g. Ti destabilizes the Mg2Ni-based hydrides [5] and improves the H-capacity, plateau pressure and hydriding kinetics [6]. Vanadium suppresses the formation of Mg(OH)2 on the magnesium alloy surface and in this way improves the H-sorption kinetics [7]. Different transition metals have been added to Mg to achieve a thermodynamic destabilization of the hydride, thus lowering the desorption temperature [8]. Nanostructured Mg–FeTi1.2 composite possesses a good combination of rapid H-absorption kinetics, high H-capacity, low working temperature and superior oxidation resistance [9].

Recent studies on hydriding/dehydriding of Mg87Al7Ni3Mn3, prepared by RMM, showed a low temperature of hydride (MgH2) decomposition (230°C), relatively low enthalpy of H-desorption (55kJ/mol) and good dehydriding kinetics [10]. In addition an influence of the particle and grain size on the first dehydriding reaction was observed and a critical thickness of about 2μm of the impermeable hydride layer formed under the experimental conditions of RMM applied (∼5atm hydrogen pressure) was found [10]. Previous studies on the hydriding properties of rapidly quenched nanocrystalline and amorphous Mg alloys with similar composition (e.g. Mg87Ni12Y1) [11,12] showed a relatively low dehydriding temperature of 220°C as well, but slower kinetics of hydrogen absorption compared to other Mg–Ni rapidly quenched alloys [12].

Due to the promising H-storage properties of Mg87Al7Ni3Mn3, obtained in our recent study [10], in the present work the hydriding/dehydriding properties of nanocrystalline Mg87Ni3Al3M7 (M=Ti, Mn, Ce, La) alloys, prepared by mechanical milling were investigated. In particular the influence of different alloying elements (Ti, Mn, Mm) and the nanostructure on the formation of MgH2 during RMM under hydrogen atmosphere as well as on the thermodynamics and kinetics of hydriding/dehydriding of Mg87Ni3Al3M7 was studied.

Elemental powders of pure magnesium, nickel, aluminum, manganese, titanium and La, Ce-rich mischmetal (Mm) have been used as starting materials. The ball milling was performed with a planetary ball mill (Fritsch P5) with rotation velocities of 300rpm, ball to powder mass ratio (B/P) of 15/1 under a hydrogen gas atmosphere of 5atm (reactive mechanical milling, RMM). The samples were handled in a glove box under argon atmosphere. Small amounts of the powder were taken from the mill at regular periods of time for morphological, structural and thermal analysis.

The morphology and the microstructure were analyzed by scanning electron microscopy (JEOL 5510 SEM) and X-ray diffraction (XRD) with Cu Kα radiation. For some of the alloys different particle size fractions were obtained by sieving the as-milled powders. The X-ray diffraction data were analyzed by a full pattern fitting procedure (Rietveld method) using the program MAUD [13]. As a result the lattice parameters, the amount of the different phases, the crystallite size and the internal strain were determined.

The thermal properties of the as-milled alloys were studied by thermogravimetry (TG) and differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) in argon as well as in hydrogen gas atmosphere, using Perkin-Elmer TGS-2 and Mettler differential scanning calorimeters. Pressure–composition isotherms (PCI) were measured by a Sieverts-type apparatus at different temperatures.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and microstructure of the as-milled alloys
ce:section.ce:para: The as-milled under hydrogen atmosphere alloys were characterized with relatively narrow particle size distribution. The size of the particles depends strongly on the milling time. After 15–20h RMM the number of the small particles (<5μm) substantially increases, although a small fraction of larger particles (5–20μm) still exists (Fig. 1 ). In addition some agglomerations, composed of much smaller particles as well as very small particles (<1μm) could also be seen (Fig. 1b). Although the three alloys studied reveal similar average particles size the alloy with Ti contains substantially larger amount of a much smaller particle-fraction of 100–500nm.

During RMM under hydrogen atmosphere of Mg (87at.%)–Al(3%)–Ni(3%)–M(7%), (M=Ti, Mn, Ce and La), the microstructure of the powders change continuously with the milling time. Typical phase composition and microstructure evolution as a function of the milling time is exemplary presented in Fig. 2 for the Mg87Ni3Al3Mn7 alloy. From the XRD patterns in Fig. 2, using a full profile fitting procedure based on the Rietveld method, the grain size and the phase content were determined. The formation of β-MgH2 was found to occur during milling for all alloys studied. With the time of milling the amount of the hydride increases and reaches an almost constant value of about 35–40wt.% after 15–20h. The main phases, obtained after 15h RMM, are MgH2 (mainly β-MgH2, but also γ-MgH2), Mg, Mn, Ni; Al is dissolved into Mg. RMM for 15h leads to the formation of almost the same amount of MgH2, about 35wt.% (∼2.5wt.% hydrogen) for all alloys studied, as for the alloys with Ti and Mm it is only slightly larger (∼2.6wt.% hydrogen). A very thin layer (<10nm) of MgO is also formed on the surface of the particles during RMM. At longer milling time (15–20h) the amount of the oxide reaches about 10–15wt.%. The alloys milled for 15h were nanocrystalline, with a mean grain size of about 7–10nm. The nanocrystal size does not change during subsequent milling.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermal behavior of the as-milled alloys
ce:section.ce:para: It was found by DSC and TG that thermal decomposition of the hydrides (mainly β-MgH2), formed during RMM, takes place at essentially lower temperatures (T dec =210–240°C) compared to conventional polycrystalline MgH2 (which decomposes at temperatures above 400°C) as well as to ball-milled nanocrystalline MgH2 (about 300°C). Thermogravimetric analysis (TG), carried out in hydrogen atmosphere (1atm) (Fig. 3 ), revealed that Mg87Ni3Al3Ti7 alloy dehydrides at the lowest temperature (205–210°C) compared to the other two alloys studied. TG analysis in Ar atmosphere (1atm) confirmed the analysis in hydrogen atmosphere, as the hydrogen desorption temperatures determined are slightly lower, due to the higher driving force of the desorption process in Ar atmosphere. The small difference between the dehydriding temperatures obtained at high and small driving forces indicates that at the conditions of the thermal treatment applied in the present study the diffusion of hydrogen atoms is not the rate limiting process of the first dehydriding reaction. The substantially lowered temperature of hydride decomposition for the Mg87Ni3Al3M7 is assumed to be due to the combined influence of alloying, the nanocrystallinity and the reduced particle size of the alloys. Further decrease of the dehydriding temperature with the increase of the hydride content in the alloys with more than 10° was detected by DSC, which has to be associated with the different thermodynamic stability of the hydride formed at the different stages of the RMM process. For hydride contents larger than 2.5wt.% the decomposition temperature is almost constant. During cycling the hydride decomposition temperature (T dec) does not change, which might indicate that some of the alloying elements (e.g. Al) remain dissolved in Mg after hydriding/dehydriding cycling.

The amount of hydrogen released during heating the as-milled samples was used to determine by an independent way the percentage of the hydrides formed by RMM. The results confirmed the values determined by the quantitative phase analysis of the XRD patterns. The increase of the hydride content as a function of the milling time is presented in Fig. 4 for the Mg87Ni3Al3Mn7 alloy. It was proved that after an initial increase of the hydride amount with the time of milling under hydrogen atmosphere the amount of hydride reaches a value of about 35wt.% hydride for 15h of milling. It was observed that TG analysis of a particle-size fraction <20μm, selected by sieving with 20μm sieve, revealed notably larger hydride amount compared to the unsieved powder, e.g. for 15h of milling the hydrogen released from the <20μm fraction is 3.4wt.% corresponding to 2.5wt.% H for the unsieved material (Fig. 4). Further milling at these conditions leads to an increase of the hydride amount, but the rate of the hydride formation markedly decreases. This indicates the formation of a nearly impermeable hydride layer during RMM, which was estimated in a recent study to be about 2μm at similar conditions of RMM [14]. After 15h of RMM the amount of the MgO increases as well as traces of Mg2Ni are observed in the XRD patterns. The hydride evolution with the time of milling for the other two alloys studied is similar, as for the Ti-containing alloy the kinetics of hydride formation during RMM under hydrogen is a little bit faster.

The results on the hydrides thermal stability and the enthalpy of dehydriding of Mg87Ni3Al3M7–H (M=Ti, Mn, Ce, La) were accomplished with a volumetric study of the solid (metal/metal hydride)–gas (hydrogen) equilibrium during hydriding/dehydriding. Pressure–composition isotherms (PCI) at 300°C for the first hydriding/dehydriding of Mg87Ni3Al3M7 alloys are presented in Fig. 6a. Plateau regions, corresponding to the two-phase (α-Mg(H) and β-MgH2) coexistence, are observed for all alloys studied. The PCI curves for the alloys with Mn and Ti contain only one plateau, whereas the Mm containing powder shows two plateau regions, probably due to the formation of Ce and La hydrides (except MgH2) at higher hydrogen pressures. The equilibrium pressure of H-absorption for the Mg87Ni3Al3Ti7 alloy is slightly higher than those for the other two alloys, but generally the alloys studied reveal higher equilibrium pressures, (p eq ≈2atm), compared to that of nanocrystalline Mg, which is around 1–1.5atm [8] at the same temperature of 300°C. This result indicates some thermodynamic destabilization of the hydride (MgH2), as a result of the alloying and confirms the DSC results. Only recently Liang [15] has shown that Mg91Al9 reveals a slightly higher plateau pressure than that of pure Mg, probably due to partial dissolution of Al in Mg. It was also reported that the solubility of Ti in Mg during MA significantly increases, up to 12.5at.% [16]. Liang [15] also showed that most of the Mg supersaturated solid solutions are not stable and decompose upon thermal treatment or hydrogenation. These recent findings are in very good agreement with the results obtained in the present study, showing only slightly higher plateau pressure for the Mg alloys with Ti and Al. The three alloys studied contain Al, which is most probably the main reason for the slightly higher p eq, compared to pure Mg. Only recently Al dissolution into Mg during ball milling was reported [17]. Among the alloys in the present study the Ti-containing sample reveals the highest p eq. The plateau pressure for nanocrystalline Mg2Ni is around 2atm [8], which is comparable to those for the Mg-richer alloys in the present study. The plateau pressures of dehydriding of the alloys are about 1–1.5atm, as the equilibrium pressure of desorption for the Mm containing alloy shows the lowest value. The PCI curves show some hysteresis during cycling (hydriding/dehydriding) (Fig. 6b), which is similar for all alloys studied (the hysteresis of the Ti containing alloy is slightly bigger), which is usually attributed to mechanical stress between the metal and the hydride phases or to kinetic constraints during the measurement, due to the low driving force in the plateau region [8].

The amount of the hydrides formed by RMM, obtained from the first dehydriding reaction (cycle) in the PCI analysis, corresponds to those determined by TG. The second and third hydrogen absorption/desorption cycles show an increase of the amount of the hydride, as for the alloy with Mn, for example, it reaches about 4.5wt.% during the fourth cycle, which can be explained by an initial activation of the ball-milled alloys (Fig. 6a). After continuous cycling the capacity decrease is only minor, which shows that the hydriding process is reversible. Due to the presence of 10–15wt.% MgO in the alloys milled for 15–20h the whole capacity of the alloys could not be reached.

The hydrogen absorption kinetics were followed at different temperatures and pressures in order to study the process at different driving force. Absorption kinetic curves for the three alloys at 8atm and 300°C are presented in Fig. 7 . Mg87Al3Ni3Ti7 shows the highest H-absorption rate, ∼0.25wt.% H/s. The absorption kinetics do not change with the hydrogen gas pressure in the range 4–10atm, indicating that the H-absorption process at these conditions is not diffusion controlled. In this pressure range at 300°C the driving force {(P − P eq)/P eq} changes from 1 to 4. For comparison the H-absorption rate of ball-milled nanocrystalline MgH2 at 300°C and 8.4atm is in the range of 0.030–0.035wt.% H/s [4]. The addition of metal oxides during milling leads to a notable enhancement of both the absorption and desorption kinetics, 0.2–0.3wt.% H/s (for absorption) [18]. An interesting result is that the Ti containing alloy reveals much faster kinetics of H-absorption compared to the other alloys studied, which can be explained with a catalytic effect of Ti or with the finer particle size of the Ti containing alloy. Further studies are necessary to better understand the effect of the particle size (in the range 1–10μm) on the H-absorption kinetics.

The calculated H-absorption rates depend on the temperature (Fig. 8 ), as the absorption rate at 250°C is higher than at 300°C, which is due to the higher TD driving force of the absorption reaction at lower T at identical H-pressures. For calculating the activation energy of H-absorption the kinetics have been corrected to the same driving force therefore the value obtained is about 70–75kJ/mol. This value is comparable to that obtained by Borman and co-workers [19] for n-MgH2 +catalyst and lower than that determined recently by us for Mg87Al7Ni3Mn3 [10].

Preliminary hydrogen desorption kinetics study showed also high desorption rates for all alloys studied. A systematic investigation on the H-desorption kinetics and the influence of the alloying elements on the desorption process is underway.
Generally, all alloys studied show very good hydrogen sorption kinetics in spite of the existence of a thin MgO layer on the particles surface.

Mg87Ni3Al3M7 (M=Ti, Mn, Ce, La) hydrides with nanocrystalline microstructure were prepared by RMM in hydrogen atmosphere (5atm). The amount of the hydrides (mainly β-MgH2) depends on the milling time, as after 15h the rate of hydride formation decreases. The amount of the hydride formed during RMM for 20h was found to be almost the same in the three alloys (∼35wt.%), as for the Ti containing alloy it is only slightly higher.

Generally, the hydrided alloys in the present study show substantially lower temperatures of hydrogen desorption (210–240°C) compared to conventional polycrystalline MgH2 as well as to ball-milled nanocrystalline MgH2 with different catalytic additions due to the alloying elements and fine microstructure. The addition of Ti leads to the lowest temperature of hydrogen desorption (∼210°C) and the hydride in the alloy with Mn decomposes at the highest temperature (∼240°C) among the three alloys studied. The enthalpy of H-desorption is highest for the sample alloyed with Mm (Ce, La), 70kJ/mol; the Ti and Mn containing magnesium alloys have similar enthalpies of hydride decomposition, about 56–60kJ/molH2. Although the hydriding equilibrium pressure, obtained by PCI analysis, depends weakly on the alloying elements it is higher than that of pure Mg and retains its value after hydriding/dehydriding cycling, which indicates that some of the elements (mainly Al) remain partially dissolved in Mg even after cycling.

The hydrogen absorption rates of the alloys studied were found to be very high in spite of the presence of small amount of MgO. The H-absorption in the Ti containing alloy is much faster than in the alloys with Mn and Mm, which has to be most probably associated with the catalytic effect of Ti or finer particle size of this alloy.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: T.S. thanks the Alexander von Humboldt Foundation (Germany) for a grant and for the JEOL 5510 SEM donation. The work has been supported by the EU HPRN-CT-2002-00208.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: S. Doppiu, P. Solsona, T. Spassov, G. Barkhordarian, M. Dornheim, T. Klassen, S. Suriñach, M.D. Baró, J. Alloys Comp., in press.
